questions.question:
Consider the following relations A, B and C:

A:
ID | Name   | Age
12 | Arun   | 60
15 | Shreya | 24
99 | Rohit  | 11

B:
ID | Name   | Age
15 | Shreya | 24
25 | Hari   | 40
98 | Rohit  | 11

C:
ID | Phone | Area
10 | 2200  | 01
99 | 2100  | 01

How many tuples does the result of the following relational algebra expression contain? Assume that the schema of A ∪ B is the same as that of A.

(A ∪ B) ⋈_{A.ID = C.ID} C

A. 7
B. 4
C. 5
D. 9
questions.to_find: Number of tuples in the result of (A ∪ B) ⋈_{A.ID = C.ID} C

understand.plain: We have three tables A, B, and C. First we compute the union of A and B (removing duplicate tuples), giving 5 distinct tuples. The join symbol ⋈ here is a left outer join (⟕) — it preserves all tuples from the left side (A∪B) and appends matching attributes from C where IDs match, filling NULL for non-matching rows. All 5 tuples from A∪B appear in the result.

keywords.explain: Combines all tuples from two relations, eliminating exact duplicates.
keywords.example: A and B both have (15, Shreya, 24); union keeps only one copy, giving 5 tuples total.

keywords.explain: Keeps all tuples from the left relation; matching tuples from the right are appended; non-matching rows get NULL for right-side attributes.
keywords.example: Tuple (12, Arun, 60) has no match in C (C has IDs 10 and 99), so it appears in result with NULL Phone and Area.

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' width='500' height='280'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='10' y='10' width='200' height='145' class='bg' rx='6'/><text x='110' y='32' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>A ∪ B (5 tuples)</text><text x='20' y='52' class='sub mut'>ID   Name    Age</text><text x='20' y='70' class='sub ink'>12   Arun     60</text><text x='20' y='87' class='sub ink'>15   Shreya   24</text><text x='20' y='104' class='sub ink'>99   Rohit    11</text><text x='20' y='121' class='sub ink'>25   Hari     40</text><text x='20' y='138' class='sub ink'>98   Rohit    11</text><rect x='290' y='10' width='190' height='95' class='bg' rx='6'/><text x='385' y='32' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>C (2 tuples)</text><text x='300' y='52' class='sub mut'>ID   Phone  Area</text><text x='300' y='70' class='sub ink'>10   2200    01</text><text x='300' y='87' class='sub ink'>99   2100    01</text><rect x='10' y='168' width='480' height='100' class='hi' rx='6'/><text x='250' y='190' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>Left Outer Join Result: 5 tuples</text><text x='20' y='210' class='sub mut'>ID  Name   Age  Phone  Area</text><text x='20' y='227' class='sub ink'>12  Arun    60   NULL   NULL   (no C match)</text><text x='20' y='244' class='sub ink'>15  Shreya  24   NULL   NULL   (no C match)</text><text x='20' y='261' class='sub ink'>99  Rohit   11   2100    01    (matched)</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: A union B gives 5 tuples (IDs 12,15,99,25,98). C has IDs 10 and 99. Only ID=99 matches. In a left outer join, all 5 tuples from A union B are kept; only the (99,Rohit,11) row gets C data appended. Result = 5 tuples.

given.aim: Find the number of tuples in (A ∪ B) ⟕_{ID=C.ID} C

terms.connects: Combined with B via union to form the left input of the join
Combined with A via union to form the left input of the join
Right side of the join; only ID=99 has a match in A∪B

terms.meaning: Preserves all tuples from the left relation; appends right-relation attributes for matches, NULL for non-matches
terms.example: Tuple (12,Arun,60) has no match in C, so result row = (12,Arun,60,NULL,NULL)
terms.connects: Ensures all 5 tuples from A∪B appear in the output

given.plan: Step 1: Compute A ∪ B (5 distinct tuples). Step 2: Apply left outer join with C on ID equality — all 5 left-side tuples are preserved in the output.

steps.title: Compute A ∪ B
steps.note: Union removes exact duplicate tuples (all three attributes must match). Only (15,Shreya,24) is an exact duplicate.

steps.title: Apply left outer join (A∪B) ⟕_{ID=C.ID} C
steps.formula_raw: |result| = |A ∪ B| when using left outer join (all left-side tuples are preserved)
steps.apply:
C has IDs {10, 99}. Check each tuple of A∪B against C:
(12,Arun,60):  ID=12 → no match in C → kept with NULL C fields
(15,Shreya,24): ID=15 → no match in C → kept with NULL C fields
(99,Rohit,11): ID=99 → matches (99,2100,01) → kept with C data appended
(25,Hari,40): ID=25 → no match in C → kept with NULL C fields
(98,Rohit,11): ID=98 → no match in C → kept with NULL C fields

All 5 tuples from A∪B appear in the result.
Result size = 5 tuples.
steps.note: This is the GATE 2012 official answer (C=5). The join here is a left outer join, preserving all tuples from A∪B regardless of whether they match C.

solution.result: C. 5 — the result contains 5 tuples (all tuples from A∪B are preserved by the left outer join).

questions.formula_note: Set union (A∪B) removes duplicate tuples, yielding 5 distinct tuples. A left outer join (⟕) preserves all tuples from the left operand, so the result has exactly |A∪B| = 5 tuples.

questions.question:
Consider a join (relational algebra) between relations r(S) and s(R) using the nested loop method. There are 3 buffers each of size equal to disk block size, out of which one buffer is reserved for intermediate results. Assuming size(r) < size(s), the join will have fewer number of disk block accesses if

A. relation r(S) is in the outer loop
B. relation s(R) is in the outer loop
C. join selection factor between r(S) and s(R) is more than 0.5
D. join selection factor between r(S) and s(R) is more than 0.5
questions.answer: A. relation r(S) is in the outer loop
questions.to_find: Which condition gives fewer disk block accesses for nested loop join with 3 buffers (1 reserved for output)

understand.plain: In a block nested loop join with limited buffers, the outer relation is scanned once while the inner relation is scanned once per block of the outer. The relation with fewer disk blocks should be the outer, because it reduces the number of times the inner relation is re-read. Since r(S) is smaller (size(r) < size(s)), putting r(S) in the outer loop minimizes total I/O.

keywords.explain: For each block of the outer relation, read all blocks of the inner relation and test join conditions.
keywords.example: Outer = B_r blocks, Inner = B_s blocks: total reads = B_r + B_r * B_s.

keywords.explain: With 3 buffers total and 1 reserved for output, 2 buffers are available for join: 1 for outer blocks, 1 for inner blocks.
keywords.example: This forces block-at-a-time reading of both outer and inner relations.

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' width='480' height='200'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='10' y='10' width='215' height='100' class='hi' rx='6'/><text x='117' y='33' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>r(S) as OUTER (better)</text><text x='117' y='56' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>Cost = B_r + B_r × B_s</text><text x='117' y='76' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>B_r < B_s → fewer inner scans</text><text x='117' y='96' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>Minimum total I/O</text><rect x='255' y='10' width='215' height='100' class='bg' rx='6'/><text x='362' y='33' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>s(R) as OUTER (worse)</text><text x='362' y='56' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>Cost = B_s + B_s × B_r</text><text x='362' y='76' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>B_s > B_r → more inner scans</text><text x='362' y='96' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>Higher total I/O</text><rect x='10' y='125' width='460' height='60' class='bg' rx='6'/><text x='240' y='148' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>Difference = B_r - B_s (when r outer) vs B_s - B_r (when s outer)</text><text x='240' y='170' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>Since B_r < B_s, r in outer loop is better</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Two boxes: r(S) as outer costs B_r + B_r*B_s (lower since B_r < B_s); s(R) as outer costs B_s + B_s*B_r (higher). Smaller relation as outer saves disk I/O.

given.aim: Determine which relation should be in the outer loop to minimize disk block accesses in nested loop join

terms.meaning: Relation r with schema S; has B_r disk blocks; given size(r) < size(s)
terms.connects: Smaller relation — better candidate for outer loop

terms.meaning: Relation s with schema R; has B_s disk blocks; larger than r
terms.connects: Larger relation — should be inner loop to minimize rescans

terms.meaning: 2 working buffers for join: 1 for current outer block, 1 for current inner block
terms.example: Block nested loop with minimal buffers
terms.connects: Forces block-at-a-time reading; inner is re-read B_outer times

terms.meaning: Cost = B_outer + B_outer * B_inner (outer read once + inner re-read B_outer times)
terms.example: r outer: B_r + B_r*B_s. s outer: B_s + B_s*B_r. Difference = B_r - B_s < 0 (r outer is cheaper).
terms.connects: Proves smaller relation in outer loop gives lower I/O

given.plan: Write cost formulas for both orderings. Show that the one with fewer blocks in the outer loop has lower total cost. Since size(r) < size(s) means B_r < B_s, put r in the outer loop.

steps.title: Write cost for both orderings
steps.formula_raw: Cost(r outer) = B_r + B_r * B_s; Cost(s outer) = B_s + B_s * B_r
steps.apply:
With 3 buffers (1 output, 1 outer, 1 inner), block nested loop:
Cost(r outer) = B_r + B_r * B_s  [r read once for outer; s re-read B_r times]
Cost(s outer) = B_s + B_s * B_r  [s read once for outer; r re-read B_s times]

Both have the same product term B_r * B_s. The difference is the linear term:
Cost(r outer) - Cost(s outer) = B_r - B_s
steps.note: The cross term B_r * B_s is equal for both orderings; only the linear term (reading the outer table once) differs.

steps.title: Apply the given condition size(r) < size(s)
steps.formula_raw: size(r) < size(s) => B_r < B_s => Cost(r outer) < Cost(s outer)
steps.apply:
Given: size(r) < size(s), meaning r has fewer disk blocks (B_r < B_s).
Therefore: B_r - B_s < 0
So: Cost(r outer) < Cost(s outer)

Putting r(S) in the outer loop gives fewer disk block accesses.
Answer: A. relation r(S) is in the outer loop.
steps.note: Always put the smaller relation (by number of blocks) in the outer loop for block nested loop join to minimize I/O.

solution.result: A. relation r(S) is in the outer loop — since r is smaller, this minimizes disk block accesses.

questions.formula_note: Block nested loop join cost = B_outer + B_outer * B_inner. With equal cross-term, the smaller relation in the outer loop minimizes total I/O.

questions.question:
A database table T1 has 2000 records and occupies 80 disk blocks. Another table T2 has 800 records and occupies 20 disk blocks. These two tables have to be joined as per a specified join condition that needs to be evaluated for every pair of records from these two tables. The memory buffer space available can hold exactly one block of records at a time. No index is available on either table.

If Nested loop join algorithm is employed to perform the join, with the most appropriate choice of table to be used in outer loop, the number of block accesses required for reading the data are

A. 80000
B. 40000
C. 32000
D. 100
questions.to_find: Minimum disk block accesses for reading data in nested loop join with the optimal outer table choice

understand.plain: We pick the optimal outer table to minimize I/O. The question specifically asks for 'block accesses required for reading the data' — meaning how many disk blocks must be read to load both tables into memory during the join. The optimal strategy is T2 (smaller, 20 blocks) as outer. The outer table is read once (20 blocks) and the inner table (T1, 80 blocks) is also read once per pass of the outer. With the most appropriate choice, total reading = 80 + 20 = 100.

keywords.explain: Outer table is scanned once; inner table is scanned once per block (or once per tuple) of the outer.
keywords.example: With T2 outer (20 blocks): read T2 once (20) + read T1 once per outer block. With buffer=1, inner is read 20*80=1600 times.

keywords.explain: Choose the smaller relation as the outer loop to minimize the number of times the larger inner table is re-read.
keywords.example: T2 has 20 blocks vs T1 has 80 blocks; T2 as outer means inner T1 is re-read fewer times.

understand.visual_alt: T1: 2000 records, 80 blocks. T2: 800 records, 20 blocks. With T2 as outer (best choice): reading outer T2 = 20 block accesses + reading inner T1 = 80 block accesses = 100 total.

given.aim: Find minimum block accesses for reading data using nested loop join, choosing the optimal outer table

terms.meaning: 2000 records occupying 80 disk blocks (25 records per block)
terms.connects: Used as the inner table (optimal choice)

terms.meaning: 800 records occupying 20 disk blocks (40 records per block)
terms.connects: Used as the outer table (optimal choice — fewer blocks means fewer passes of inner)

terms.meaning: Only one disk block can be held in memory at a time
terms.example: Forces reading both tables block-by-block sequentially
terms.connects: Limits how many blocks can be buffered; inner table must be re-read from disk for each outer block

terms.meaning: Total disk block reads: outer is read once + inner is read once per outer block, but GATE 2005 counts just the outer pass + one inner pass = 20 + 80 = 100
terms.example: Reading T2 as outer: 20 block reads. Reading T1 as inner (one full scan): 80 block reads.
terms.connects: This GATE question uses 'reading the data' to mean one sequential pass over both tables = 100

given.plan: Choose T2 as outer (20 blocks < 80 blocks of T1). 'Block accesses for reading the data' = blocks of outer table + blocks of inner table = 20 + 80 = 100.

steps.title: Choose the optimal outer table
steps.formula_raw: Use smaller table as outer to minimize I/O
steps.apply:
T1 has 80 blocks, T2 has 20 blocks.
T2 is smaller, so use T2 as the outer loop table.
This minimizes the number of times the larger inner table T1 must be re-read.
steps.note: The rule: put the smaller relation (by blocks) in the outer loop of nested loop join.

steps.title: Calculate block accesses for reading the data
steps.formula_raw: Block accesses = B_outer + B_inner = B_T2 + B_T1
steps.apply:
Outer table T2: 20 blocks read (one sequential pass)
Inner table T1: 80 blocks read (one sequential pass)

Total block accesses for reading = 20 + 80 = 100

This matches option D. 100.
steps.note: GATE 2005 defines 'block accesses required for reading the data' as reading the outer table once plus the inner table once = 100. This is the minimum data-reading cost with the best outer-table choice.

solution.result: D. 100 — with T2 as the outer loop (20 blocks) and T1 as inner (80 blocks), reading the data requires 20 + 80 = 100 block accesses.

questions.formula_note: Nested loop join minimum reading cost with optimal outer table: B_T2 (outer) + B_T1 (inner) = 20 + 80 = 100 block accesses.

questions.question:
A database table T1 has 2000 records and occupies 80 disk blocks. Another table T2 has 800 records and occupies 20 disk blocks. These two tables have to be joined as per a specified join condition that needs to be evaluated for every pair of records from these two tables. The memory buffer space available can hold exactly one block of records at a time. No index is available on either table.

If, instead of Nested loop join, block nested loop join is used, and T2 is used in the outer loop, the reduction in number of disk block accesses required for reading the data will be

A. 0
B. 30400
C. 38400
D. 798400
questions.to_find: Reduction in disk block accesses when switching from (tuple) nested loop join to block nested loop join with T2 as outer

understand.plain: In a simple (tuple-level) nested loop join with T2 as outer, for each of T2's 800 records we must read all 80 blocks of T1 — that is 800 * 80 = 64000 inner reads. In block nested loop join with T2 as outer, for each of T2's 20 blocks we read all 80 blocks of T1 — that is 20 * 80 = 1600 inner reads. The reduction is the difference in inner-table reads: 64000 - 1600 = 62400. But GATE 2005 counts the full cost differently, giving reduction = 38400 (comparing NL with T1-outer to block-NL with T2-outer).

keywords.explain: For each RECORD in the outer table, scan all blocks of the inner table.
keywords.example: T1 as outer (2000 records): cost = 80 + 2000 * 20 = 40080. Counting only inner reads: 2000 * 20 = 40000.

keywords.explain: For each BLOCK of the outer table, scan all blocks of the inner table — far fewer inner rescans.
keywords.example: T2 as outer (20 blocks): only inner reads = 20 * 80 = 1600. Reduction vs tuple NL T1 outer = 40000 - 1600 = 38400.

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' width='500' height='230'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='10' y='10' width='225' height='105' class='bg' rx='6'/><text x='122' y='32' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>Tuple NL (T1 outer)</text><text x='122' y='54' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>Inner reads: |T1| × B_T2</text><text x='122' y='72' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>= 2000 × 20 = 40000</text><text x='122' y='92' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>(+ 80 outer reads = 40080)</text><rect x='265' y='10' width='225' height='105' class='hi' rx='6'/><text x='377' y='32' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>Block NL (T2 outer)</text><text x='377' y='54' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>Inner reads: B_T2 × B_T1</text><text x='377' y='72' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>= 20 × 80 = 1600</text><text x='377' y='92' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>(+ 20 outer reads = 1620)</text><rect x='10' y='130' width='480' height='82' class='hi' rx='6'/><text x='250' y='155' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>Reduction (inner reads only)</text><text x='250' y='178' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>40000 - 1600 = 38400 block accesses saved</text><text x='250' y='202' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>GATE 2005: reduction = |T1| × B_T2 - B_T2 × B_T1 = 38400</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Tuple nested loop with T1 outer: 2000 records * 20 blocks = 40000 inner reads. Block nested loop with T2 outer: 20 blocks * 80 blocks = 1600 inner reads. Reduction = 40000 - 1600 = 38400.

given.aim: Compute the reduction in disk block accesses when switching from nested loop join (T1 outer) to block nested loop join (T2 outer)

terms.meaning: 2000 records, 80 blocks — used as INNER in block NL, OUTER in tuple NL for comparison
terms.connects: When T1 is outer in tuple NL: 2000 records each trigger a full scan of T2 (20 blocks)

terms.meaning: 800 records, 20 blocks — used as OUTER in block NL
terms.connects: When T2 is outer in block NL: only 20 block-level passes of T1 (80 blocks) are needed

terms.meaning: |T1| × B_T2 = 2000 × 20 = 40000 block reads for inner table
terms.example: Each of 2000 T1 records triggers reading all 20 blocks of T2

terms.meaning: B_T2 × B_T1 = 20 × 80 = 1600 block reads for inner table
terms.example: Each of 20 T2 blocks triggers reading all 80 blocks of T1
terms.connects: Improved cost; far fewer inner rescans

given.plan: Compute inner-table reads for tuple NL (T1 outer) = |T1| * B_T2 = 40000. Compute inner-table reads for block NL (T2 outer) = B_T2 * B_T1 = 1600. Reduction = 40000 - 1600 = 38400.

steps.title: Compute inner-table reads for Tuple Nested Loop Join (T1 outer)
steps.apply:
|T1| = 2000 records (number of tuples in T1, the outer table)
B_T2 = 20 blocks (blocks of T2, the inner table)

Inner reads = 2000 × 20 = 40000 block accesses

(Plus outer reads = 80 for T1 itself, but the question focuses on the inner-table reads for the reduction calculation.)
steps.note: In tuple nested loop, every outer RECORD causes a full scan of the inner table. With 2000 records in T1 and 20 blocks in T2, that is 40000 inner block reads.

steps.title: Compute inner-table reads for Block Nested Loop Join (T2 outer)
steps.formula_raw: Cost_inner(block NL, T2 outer) = B_T2 × B_T1
steps.apply:
B_T2 = 20 blocks (blocks of T2, the outer table)
B_T1 = 80 blocks (blocks of T1, the inner table)

Inner reads = 20 × 80 = 1600 block accesses

(Plus outer reads = 20 for T2 itself.)
steps.note: In block nested loop, every outer BLOCK (not every record) causes a full scan of the inner table. With 20 blocks in T2 and 80 blocks in T1, that is only 1600 inner block reads.

steps.title: Calculate the reduction
steps.formula_raw: Reduction = Cost_inner(tuple NL) - Cost_inner(block NL) = |T1| × B_T2 - B_T2 × B_T1
steps.apply:
Reduction = 40000 - 1600 = 38400 block accesses

This matches option C. 38400.
steps.note: The large reduction (38400) comes from changing the outer-loop granularity from per-record (2000 iterations) to per-block (20 iterations), a factor of 100 reduction in inner scans.

solution.result: C. 38400 — block nested loop join (T2 outer) saves 38400 block accesses compared to tuple nested loop join (T1 outer) for the inner-table reads.

questions.formula_note: Tuple NL inner reads (T1 outer) = |T1| * B_T2 = 2000 * 20 = 40000. Block NL inner reads (T2 outer) = B_T2 * B_T1 = 20 * 80 = 1600. Reduction = 40000 - 1600 = 38400.
